prompts.title: Structured Abstract for Q1 Journal
prompts.prompt: Act as a research writer with 12 years of publishing in Q1 Scopus / WoS journals. Write a 250-word structured abstract for my paper. I will paste the manuscript. Use IMRaD structure with sub-headers (Background, Methods, Results, Conclusions) and end with a 'Significance' line for editor screening. Highlight: (1) the gap addressed, (2) novelty in 1 sentence, (3) sample/dataset/methodology in 2 lines, (4) 3 quantitative findings (effect sizes + p-values where applicable), (5) practical implication for Indian context. Provide 6 keywords (mix of broad + niche, all from MeSH or UGC-CARE thesaurus). Constraints: avoid jargon-heavy first sentence; quantify every claim; cap each section to its allotted word range; submit-ready.

prompts.title: Cover Letter to Editor-in-Chief
prompts.prompt: Act as a publication strategist with 10 years of guiding Indian researchers. Draft a 280-word cover letter to the Editor-in-Chief of a target journal (specify type: Elsevier / Springer / Nature subsidiary / SAGE / Taylor & Francis Indian editions). Letter should: (1) open with manuscript title + submission type (research article / brief / review), (2) state core contribution in 60 words (what is novel, why important now), (3) explain fit with the journal's scope (cite 2 recent papers from the journal), (4) confirm originality + non-concurrent submission + ethics approval (where applicable, ICMR / DPDP), (5) suggest 4 reviewer names (with affiliation + email + 1-line rationale) avoiding conflict of interest, (6) declare funding source + COI, (7) sign-off with corresponding author contact. Format as a ready-to-submit letter. Constraints: avoid generic praise; submit-style polite; respect blind-review where applicable.

prompts.title: Response to Reviewer Comments (Revision)
prompts.prompt: Act as a senior researcher with 14 years across 90+ peer reviews. I will paste 3 reviewer reports + the editor's decision (major revision). Draft my point-by-point response letter. For each reviewer comment: (1) restate the comment verbatim in italics, (2) thank the reviewer warmly, (3) describe the change made (with line + page reference in revised manuscript), (4) if not making the change, explain politely with a defensible argument and citations, (5) for analytical disputes, present new analyses with evidence. Conclude with a summary of all major changes (250 words). Format as a structured response document. Constraints: never argue defensively; agree with > 70% of reviewer suggestions; if reviewers disagree, navigate diplomatically; submit-ready PDF.

prompts.title: Predatory Journal Filter for Indian Authors
prompts.prompt: Act as a research integrity advisor at INFLIBNET. Build a checklist for Indian PhD scholars to filter predatory journals before submission. Cover 18 red-flag indicators across: (1) editorial transparency (named editors, affiliations, COI), (2) peer-review claims (turnaround impossibly fast, no double-blind), (3) impact factor source (Web of Science / Scopus vs fake metrics), (4) publication fees (excessive APC, hidden charges), (5) UGC-CARE List I/II inclusion verification, (6) DOAJ membership, (7) website quality (typos, broken links, fake stats), (8) email solicitation patterns, (9) indexing claims that don't match reality, (10) Beall's-list legacy + Cabells shortlist. Provide a step-by-step verification SOP plus an INR-budget conscious alternative list of legitimate Indian + global open-access venues. Format as a researcher's safety checklist. Constraints: align with UGC notice + Cabells whitelist; explain APC norms (₹0-₹2L range); protect early-career scholars.

prompts.title: Highlights Section for Cell-Style Journal
prompts.prompt: Act as a publication coach with 8 years of writing for Cell Press / Elsevier journals that require a 'Highlights' section. I will paste my paper. Write 4 highlight bullets, each ≤85 characters, that capture: (1) novelty, (2) method advance, (3) key result with effect size, (4) implication. Plus produce a 50-word 'In Brief' summary suited for graphical-abstract narration. Format as a structured highlights block + In Brief block. Constraints: each bullet must stand alone for non-expert reader; quantify all claims; avoid jargon; mirror the journal's recent published highlights tone.

prompts.title: Conflict-of-Interest Disclosure Statement
prompts.prompt: Act as a research compliance officer. Draft a complete COI + funding + ethics + author-contributions disclosure block for my paper (Indian co-authors only). Provide: (1) author contributions per CRediT taxonomy (14 roles mapped to each author), (2) funding statement (named grant + agency + grant number — SERB/DST/ICMR/CSIR), (3) data availability statement (Mendeley / Zenodo / restricted with rationale), (4) code availability statement (GitHub link, license), (5) ethics approval statement (institutional IRB, registration number, date), (6) consent for participation + publication, (7) competing interests (financial, non-financial, institutional), (8) acknowledgments of non-author contributors. Format as a journal-ready disclosure block. Constraints: align with ICMJE recommendations + UGC ordinance; full transparency; no boilerplate.

prompts.title: Letter to Editor Appealing Rejection
prompts.prompt: Act as a senior PI who has appealed 3 rejections successfully in past 5 years. Draft a 350-word respectful appeal letter to the Editor-in-Chief. The decision was 'reject' but reviewer 2 made factual errors. Letter should: (1) thank the editor for the consideration + reviewer effort, (2) state the basis of appeal (specific factual / methodological misunderstanding by reviewer 2 — point-by-point), (3) attach 1-page evidence rebuttal, (4) request second-opinion review by a different reviewer, (5) propose revisions willing to make if reconsideration is granted, (6) acknowledge the editor's right to maintain rejection, (7) provide alternative outcome ask (resubmit as new ms vs invited revision), (8) sign off respectfully. Format as a polished appeal. Constraints: never attack the reviewer's integrity; cite COPE guidelines + journal's appeal policy; prepare for graceful outcome regardless.

prompts.title: Open-Access vs Subscription Journal Decision
prompts.prompt: Act as an open-access advocate at IIT Bombay library. Help a PhD scholar choose between submitting to (a) a Q1 subscription journal (no APC, embargo 12 months), (b) a Q1 open-access journal (APC ₹2.4L), (c) a green-OA repository (free + paywall journal). Build a decision framework covering: (1) reach + impact metrics for each option, (2) compliance with funder mandates (SERB, ICMR, DBT, Wellcome Trust India Alliance — UGC-CARE), (3) APC affordability + waiver options, (4) author rights retention (CC BY vs publisher copyright), (5) embargo implications for citations, (6) preprint policy (compatibility), (7) repository options (NDLI, ShodhGanga, IndiaRxiv), (8) recommendation matrix scored 1-10 across 7 axes. Format as a decision-support memo. Constraints: align with UGC's open-access push; honest about APC affordability for Indian scholars; cite Plan-S principles where applicable.

prompts.title: Pre-Print Posting Strategy on IndiaRxiv
prompts.prompt: Act as an open-science advocate. Build a pre-print posting strategy for my submission-ready manuscript before sending it to a Q1 journal. Cover: (1) repository selection (arXiv / SSRN / IndiaRxiv / OSF — pros, cons, fit), (2) versioning policy (v1 pre-submission, v2 after major revision), (3) license (CC BY 4.0 vs more restrictive), (4) embargo + double-anonymous-friendly wording, (5) cross-posting strategy with social (Twitter / LinkedIn / Mastodon), (6) dataset + code linking from preprint, (7) DOI assignment, (8) ORCID linking, (9) institutional repository copy. Format as a posting-strategy playbook. Constraints: respect target journal's pre-print policy (Sherpa Romeo lookup); preserve double-blind review eligibility; minimal redaction needed; cite UGC IndiaRxiv collaboration if relevant.

prompts.title: Research Impact Statement for Promotion
prompts.prompt: Act as a faculty career coach with 11 years of advising Indian assistant professors going up for promotion. Write a 700-word research impact statement based on my CV (which I will paste). Statement should cover: (1) overarching research narrative (what 5-year arc connects my publications), (2) top 3 contributions with citation counts + practical adoption examples, (3) evidence of impact (industry partnerships with TCS / Infosys / pharma / government policy briefs), (4) student mentorship (PhD scholars graduated, mentored), (5) grants secured (₹ Cr value), (6) editorial / reviewer service, (7) trajectory for next 5 years (vision + planned grants), (8) closing why-promote line. Format as a polished promotion document. Constraints: honest not inflated; quantify everything; cite UGC promotion criteria + AICTE/NAAC where applicable; avoid 'first-ever' grandstanding.

prompts.title: ORCID + Scholar Profile Setup Guide
prompts.prompt: Act as an academic identity coach. Build a 1-page setup guide for an Indian PhD scholar's ORCID, Google Scholar, ResearchGate, and Scopus author profile. For each: (1) account creation steps, (2) profile completeness checklist, (3) auto-link to publications, (4) duplicate-merge process for confused author IDs, (5) co-author + affiliation linking, (6) Indian-specific fields (PAN-mapped name, university affiliation history), (7) privacy settings (DPDP-compliant), (8) cross-platform sync workflow, (9) maintenance routine (monthly), (10) impact-metric tracking dashboard (h-index, citations). Format as a setup-and-maintenance playbook. Constraints: free tier; no fake-co-author requests; align with UGC researcher-identity push; integration with INFLIBNET / IRINS.

prompts.title: Tweet/X Thread to Promote Published Paper
prompts.prompt: Act as a research-communication strategist with 6 years helping Indian academics broaden reach. Write a 9-tweet X (Twitter) thread to promote my newly published paper. I will paste the abstract + 2 key figures. Thread should: (1) hook tweet (1 line: the surprising finding), (2) tweet 2-3: context + why this matters now, (3) tweet 4-5: methodology in plain language, (4) tweet 6-7: the key results with one chart per tweet, (5) tweet 8: implication for Indian policy / industry / society, (6) tweet 9: link to paper + co-authors tagged + handles + grant credit. Provide also: (a) LinkedIn post version (250 words), (b) Mastodon / Bluesky-friendly variant, (c) WhatsApp university group blurb (60 words). Format as ready-to-post copy. Constraints: avoid hyperbole; one CTA per tweet; cite OA link if available; tag funders.

prompts.title: Citation Behavior Analysis (Self + Peer)
prompts.prompt: Act as a bibliometrician with 9 years of helping Indian researchers analyse citation patterns. I will paste my citation profile from Scopus + Google Scholar. Provide: (1) overall trajectory (h-index, i10-index, total citations, year-on-year growth), (2) top 5 cited papers + analysis why they cited well, (3) self-citation ratio (and ICMJE-acceptable threshold), (4) field-normalised citation index (vs IIT/IIM peer benchmark), (5) collaboration network (top 10 co-citers + countries), (6) altmetric vs citation correlation, (7) early-impact predictor for recent papers, (8) recommended action (submit which paper to which higher-impact venue next). Format as a bibliometric report. Constraints: honest about weak years; respect privacy; align with UGC's emerging research evaluation framework; benchmark with relevant comparable Indian + global authors.

prompts.title: Special-Issue Proposal to Editor
prompts.prompt: Act as a guest editor with 7 years of curating special issues for Q1 journals. Draft a 600-word special-issue proposal to submit to a target journal's Editor-in-Chief. Topic: 'AI for Public Health in India: Equity, Implementation, and Governance'. Proposal should include: (1) topical urgency (India's public-health-AI moment), (2) gap addressed (180 words), (3) proposed scope (8 article types: original, perspective, case study, methodology, ethics, policy brief), (4) target authors (named 12 potential contributors across IIT, AIIMS, IIM, ICMR, MoHFW), (5) timeline (call out, abstract review, full submission, peer review, publication), (6) editorial team (3 guest editors with bio + affiliation), (7) expected impact + altmetric reach, (8) special-issue marketing (conferences, webinars, social, India-press). Format as a proposal document. Constraints: align with target journal's scope + recent editorial direction; pitch a topic with policy + commercial relevance; commit to rigorous peer review.

prompts.title: Rapid Communication Brief (Letter Format)
prompts.prompt: Act as a senior researcher publishing in rapid-communication formats (IEEE Letters, ACS Letters, JBC short reports). Write a 1,200-word rapid-communication letter for my new finding (I'll paste). Structure: (1) opening (120 words: problem + gap + finding), (2) experimental approach (220 words), (3) results (450 words: 1-2 figures with concise legends), (4) discussion (250 words: comparison with prior work + 1 mechanism speculation), (5) conclusion + future-work (120 words), (6) compact references (≤25). Constraints: word cap is rigid; figures sized for 2-column letter format; novelty must justify rapid format (timeliness + impact); avoid speculative 'we propose' without data.

prompts.title: Image Manipulation Self-Check
prompts.prompt: Act as a research integrity officer with 10 years of investigating image manipulation in life-sciences journals. Build a self-check protocol for my paper's 8 figures (Western blots, gel images, microscopy, histograms). Cover: (1) raw vs final-figure trail (locked PSDs, exported TIFFs), (2) brightness/contrast acceptability (whole-image vs spot adjustment red-flag), (3) duplications + splices (no internal lane copy-paste), (4) auto-tools to use (ImageJ + Adobe inspection layers + 'Imagetwin' + COPE-recommended), (5) raw-data archival policy (GitHub LFS / Zenodo with DOI), (6) blot quantification methodology, (7) figure-legend transparency rules, (8) co-author sign-off form, (9) journal-specific image guidelines (Cell, Nature, Indian Journal of Medical Research). Format as a self-check audit + sign-off. Constraints: align with COPE + ORI; preserve raw images for 10 years; one-page final declaration form.

prompts.title: Author-Order Decision Memo
prompts.prompt: Act as an academic fairness mediator with 8 years navigating Indian co-author disputes. I will paste a draft author list with contributions claimed by each. Help me draft a transparent author-order decision memo. Cover: (1) CRediT taxonomy mapping per author (14 roles), (2) contribution score (qualitative + quantitative — analysis hours, manuscript drafting %, conceptualisation), (3) order rationale (first author drove study, last author supervised, alphabetical for equal contributions middle), (4) co-first-author justification (where applicable), (5) corresponding-author choice rationale, (6) acknowledgments for non-authors who contributed, (7) sign-off form for all authors confirming agreement, (8) conflict-resolution clause if reverted. Format as a 1-page decision memo + signed sign-off. Constraints: align with ICMJE authorship criteria (4 conditions); transparent enough that an external auditor could verify; honour seniority but not over senior people.

prompts.title: Editor Reviewer-Recruitment Email
prompts.prompt: Act as an Associate Editor for a Q1 journal who must recruit 3 reviewers for a fresh submission. Draft 3 personalised invitation emails: (1) to a senior Indian academic (IIT/IIM faculty), (2) to a global expert from a recent paper, (3) to an early-career postdoc with relevant niche expertise. Each email should: (a) name the reviewer specifically and reference one of their relevant papers, (b) provide manuscript title + abstract, (c) state reviewer effort (≤15 hours, single round), (d) deadline (3 weeks), (e) ICMJE confidentiality + COI ask, (f) compensation (CME credits / acknowledgment / reviewer credit on Publons), (g) opt-out clause graceful. Format as 3 ready-to-send emails. Constraints: never overload one expert; ensure global + Indian + ECR balance; maintain double-blind anonymity; align with COPE peer-review ethics.

prompts.title: Plain-Language Summary for Lay Audience
prompts.prompt: Act as a science communicator who writes for The Wire Science / Mongabay India / Scroll Science. Write a 350-word plain-language summary of my technical paper (I'll paste abstract + 1 figure). Structure: (1) curiosity hook (50 words: a relatable Indian scenario), (2) what we did (90 words), (3) what we found (120 words: numbers in everyday units), (4) why this matters for everyday Indian (60 words), (5) limitations honestly stated (30 words). Plus: (a) headline (≤80 chars), (b) social pull-quote (≤30 words), (c) editor pitch line (≤45 words). Format as a publish-ready piece. Constraints: 8th-grade English readability; one analogy that makes sense in Indian context; avoid 'breakthrough' / 'first-ever' overclaim; cite the paper at the end with DOI.

prompts.title: Retraction or Correction Request Letter
prompts.prompt: Act as a research integrity officer experienced with COPE-aligned retractions. I have discovered a calculation error in a published paper that affects one of three main results. Draft a corrigendum / partial-retraction request letter to the journal's Editor-in-Chief. Letter should cover: (1) opening + manuscript identification, (2) clear statement of the error + how identified, (3) impact on results (which conclusion changes, which holds), (4) proposed remediation (corrigendum vs partial retraction vs full retraction — recommend with rationale), (5) revised analysis (with corrected numbers + figure), (6) authors agreement (named all + sign-off), (7) institutional integrity-office notification, (8) post-retraction outreach (citing authors, conference talks, classroom usage). Format as a formal letter + corrigendum draft. Constraints: align with COPE retraction guidelines + ICMJE; full transparency; protect dignity while owning mistake; expedite to minimise downstream citations.
